clean_story:
Pressed letters. Silent commands.

The name computer keyboard comes from its ancestor—the typewriter’s "board of keys," a grid of levers that struck inked metal arms onto paper. Now, it strikes invisible signals instead.

A keyboard is a translator: fingers press, circuits close, and binary pulses race to the machine. Each key is a tiny switch—some use rubber domes (like pressing a bubble wrap sheet), others mechanical springs (like snapping a light switch). The QWERTY layout, born in 1873, wasn’t designed for speed but to prevent old typewriter bars from jamming. Yet it persists, even as thumbs tap glass screens.

Gamers demand keys that trigger at 1.5 millimeters; programmers rely on tactile bumps to type without looking. A single keystroke takes 10–20 milliseconds to register—faster than a hummingbird’s wingbeat.

Three related subjects are touchscreen latency, Braille input devices, and how Morse code shaped typing.
